lessons.title: Friis & the dB Link Budget
lessons.content:
A **link budget** is just bookkeeping: every gain adds and every loss subtracts until you arrive at the received power. The physics behind it is the **Friis transmission equation**, which for free space gives received power as

$$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2.$$

The term $\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ is the inverse of **free-space path loss** (FSPL). Note it scales with $\lambda^2$, not $\lambda$ — higher frequencies suffer more loss for a fixed antenna *gain*, a fact mmWave planners live with.

Multiplying ratios is tedious, so RF engineers work in **decibels**, where multiplication becomes addition. Taking $10\log_{10}$ of Friis turns it into a clean sum and difference:

$$P_r(\text{dBm}) = \underbrace{P_t + G_t}_{\text{EIRP}} + G_r - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{misc}}.$$

Here **EIRP** (effective isotropic radiated power) bundles transmit power and transmit antenna gain; $L_{\text{misc}}$ collects connector, feedline, atmospheric, and pointing losses. FSPL in dB is

$$\text{FSPL(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45,\quad d\text{ in km}, f\text{ in MHz}.$$

The key dB unit discipline: $\text{dBm}$ and $\text{dBW}$ are *absolute* power references; $\text{dBi}$ is gain over isotropic; plain $\text{dB}$ is a *ratio*. You can add a dB to a dBm but never two dBm together. As a radar tech you read VSWR and power off meters in these units daily — this is the same arithmetic scaled up to a whole link.

items.front: Write the Friis transmission equation in its power-ratio form.
items.back: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$. The bracketed term is the reciprocal of free-space path loss.

items.front: What is EIRP and how is it computed in dB?
items.back: Effective Isotropic Radiated Power: the power an isotropic radiator would need to match your transmitter's peak gain. $\text{EIRP} = P_t(\text{dBm}) + G_t(\text{dBi}) - L_{\text{feed}}$.

items.front: Why does free-space path loss increase with frequency at fixed antenna gain?
items.back: Friis carries a $\lambda^2$ factor in the received power. As $f$ rises, $\lambda$ shrinks, so the effective aperture of a fixed-gain antenna shrinks and FSPL (proportional to $(4\pi d/\lambda)^2$) grows.

items.front: Distinguish dBm, dBW, dBi, and dB.
items.back: dBm/dBW are absolute power (ref 1 mW / 1 W). dBi is antenna gain relative to an isotropic radiator. Plain dB is a dimensionless ratio (gain or loss). Add dB to dBm; never add two dBm.

items.prompt: Worked budget: $P_t = 10$ dBW, $G_t = 20$ dBi, feed loss 2 dB, FSPL = 162 dB, $G_r = 40$ dBi, atmospheric loss 3 dB. What is $P_r$?
items.explanation: EIRP = 10 + 20 - 2 = 28 dBW. $P_r$ = EIRP + $G_r$ - FSPL - atmos = 28 + 40 - 162 - 3 = -97 dBW.

items.prompt: Doubling the link distance $d$ changes FSPL by approximately:
items.explanation: FSPL $\propto d^2$, so doubling $d$ multiplies loss by 4, and $10\log_{10}(4) \approx 6$ dB.

items.prompt: Which operation is INVALID in dB bookkeeping?
choices: Adding two dBm power levels together
Adding a dBi gain to a dBm power
Subtracting a dB loss from a dBm power
Adding a dB gain to a dBW power
items.explanation: dBm is a logarithmic absolute power; adding two of them implies multiplying two powers, which is meaningless. Gains/losses (dB, dBi) add to or subtract from an absolute level.

items.prompt: In the dB form $P_r = \text{EIRP} + G_r - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{misc}}$, what does $L_{\text{misc}}$ typically include?
choices: Connector, feedline, atmospheric, rain, and pointing losses
items.explanation: $L_{\text{misc}}$ collects all the incidental losses beyond free-space spreading. Noise and required SNR enter later, when comparing $P_r$ against sensitivity.

items.prompt: FSPL(dB) = $20\log_{10} d + 20\log_{10} f + 32.45$ (d in km, f in MHz). At 2400 MHz over 10 km, FSPL is approximately:

lessons.title: Thermal Noise, Noise Figure & Sensitivity
lessons.content:
A signal is only useful relative to the **noise floor**. The fundamental floor is **thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise**, the available noise power from any resistor:

$$N = kTB,$$

where $k = 1.38\times10^{-23}$ J/K is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ the temperature in kelvin, and $B$ the bandwidth in Hz. At the standard reference $T_0 = 290$ K, the floor in dBm is the number every receiver engineer memorizes:

$$N(\text{dBm}) = -174 + 10\log_{10}B.$$

So a 1 Hz bandwidth floor is $-174$ dBm, and a 1 MHz channel is $-174 + 60 = -114$ dBm.

Real receivers add their own noise. **Noise figure** $F$ is the factor by which a stage degrades SNR: $F = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}}/\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}$, equivalently expressed as an **equivalent noise temperature** $T_e = (F-1)T_0$. For a cascade, the **Friis noise formula** gives the system noise figure:

$$F_{\text{sys}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2-1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3-1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$

The lesson is decisive: once the **first stage (the LNA)** has high gain $G_1$, later stages are divided down and contribute little. This is *why* the LNA sits first, right at the antenna — exactly the front-end logic of a radar receiver.

Putting it together, the **receiver sensitivity** (minimum detectable signal) is

$$S_{\min}(\text{dBm}) = -174 + 10\log_{10}B + F(\text{dB}) + \text{SNR}_{\min}.$$

The link only closes if $P_r > S_{\min}$.

items.front: State the thermal noise power equation and the value of $k$.
items.back: $N = kTB$, with $k = 1.38\times10^{-23}$ J/K, $T$ in kelvin, $B$ in Hz. It is independent of frequency (white) and of the resistor value.

items.front: What is the thermal noise floor in dBm at 290 K?
items.back: $N(\text{dBm}) = -174 + 10\log_{10}B$. The $-174$ dBm/Hz is $10\log_{10}(kT_0)$ converted to dBm at $T_0 = 290$ K.

items.front: Define noise figure F and its relation to equivalent noise temperature.
items.back: $F = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}}/\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}$ (the SNR degradation of a stage). Equivalent noise temperature $T_e = (F-1)T_0$, with $T_0 = 290$ K.

items.front: Write the Friis cascade noise formula and state its main implication.
items.back: $F_{\text{sys}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2-1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3-1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$. With a high-gain low-noise first stage, the first stage (LNA) dominates the system noise figure.

items.prompt: kTB calc: compute the thermal noise floor in a $B = 10$ MHz bandwidth at 290 K.
Cascade: $F_1 = 2$ dB, $G_1 = 20$ dB LNA; $F_2 = 10$ dB mixer. Why is system NF only slightly above 2 dB?
choices: The mixer's excess noise (F2-1) is divided by the LNA gain G1=100
The mixer noise is added directly with no scaling
items.explanation: Friis: $F_{\text{sys}} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1$. In linear terms $F_1 \approx 1.58$, $(F_2-1)/G_1 = (10-1)/100 = 0.09$, so $F_{\text{sys}} \approx 1.67$ (≈2.2 dB). The high LNA gain suppresses later stages.

items.prompt: A receiver has NF = 5 dB, $B = 1$ MHz, and needs $\text{SNR}_{\min} = 12$ dB. Its sensitivity is:
If you halve the receiver bandwidth, the thermal noise floor changes by:
items.explanation: $N \propto B$, so halving $B$ gives $10\log_{10}(0.5) \approx -3$ dB. Narrower bandwidth = lower noise floor = better sensitivity (at the cost of data rate or pulse resolution).

items.prompt: Why is the LNA placed first in the chain, right at the antenna?
choices: Its gain divides down the noise contribution of every following stage
items.explanation: By the Friis cascade formula, only the first stage's noise enters undivided; a high-gain low-noise first stage sets the system noise figure and minimizes signal degradation before lossy stages.

lessons.title: SNR, Margin, Interference & the Radar Range Equation
lessons.content:
With $P_r$ and the noise floor in hand, the deliverable metric is **signal-to-noise ratio**, $\text{SNR} = P_r - N$ in dB. For digital links the cleaner figure is **energy-per-bit to noise-density**, $E_b/N_0$, related to SNR by

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \text{SNR}\cdot\frac{B}{R_b},$$

where $R_b$ is the bit rate. Each modulation/coding scheme needs a minimum $E_b/N_0$ to hit a target BER (e.g. BPSK needs about 9.6 dB for $10^{-5}$ uncoded). The amount by which the actual SNR exceeds the required value is the **link margin**; the slice reserved specifically for fading and rain is the **fade margin**. Margin buys availability — the fraction of time the link stays up.

Signals also compete with other signals. **C/N** is carrier-to-noise; **C/I** is carrier-to-interference. When $I \gg N$ the link is **interference-limited** (adding transmit power helps nobody, since interferers scale too); when $N \gg I$ it is **noise-limited** (more power or lower NF helps). Co-channel and adjacent-channel interference, intermod, and jamming all enter through C/I — in radar this is the **jamming-to-signal ratio J/S**, countered by spread-spectrum processing gain.

Finally, the payoff for the radar tech: the **radar range equation** is a Friis link budget folded on itself with a target in the middle:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}.$$

The one-way $1/R^2$ spreading happens twice (out and back), giving $1/R^4$; the antenna gain appears squared (same aperture transmits and receives); and the target's radar cross-section $\sigma$ replaces the receive antenna. Set $P_r = S_{\min}$ and solve for $R$ to get detection range — the same sensitivity arithmetic from Lesson 2.

items.back: $\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \text{SNR}\cdot\frac{B}{R_b}$, where $B$ is bandwidth and $R_b$ the bit rate. $E_b/N_0$ normalizes out bandwidth and rate so modulations can be compared at a fixed BER.

items.front: Define link margin and fade margin.
items.back: Link margin = actual SNR (or $E_b/N_0$) minus the required value. Fade margin is the portion of that margin set aside to ride out fading/rain, which directly sets link availability.

items.front: Contrast a noise-limited and an interference-limited link.
items.back: Noise-limited ($N \gg I$): performance set by thermal noise; more TX power or lower NF helps. Interference-limited ($I \gg N$): set by C/I; raising power doesn't help because interferers scale too — you need filtering, spatial nulling, or coordination.

items.front: Why does the radar range equation have $1/R^4$ and $G^2$?
items.back: The wave spreads as $1/R^2$ outbound and again $1/R^2$ on the echo return, giving $1/R^4$. The same antenna transmits and receives, so its gain enters squared ($G^2$). RCS $\sigma$ replaces the receive antenna's capture.

items.prompt: $P_r = -97$ dBm and the noise floor $N = -104$ dBm. The SNR is:
A link needs $\text{SNR}_{\min} = 12$ dB and achieves 18 dB. The link margin is:
items.explanation: Margin = achieved - required = 18 - 12 = 6 dB. This is the headroom against fades before the link drops below threshold.

items.prompt: In the radar range equation $P_r = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$, doubling range $R$ reduces $P_r$ by:
items.explanation: $P_r \propto 1/R^4$, so doubling $R$ divides power by 16, i.e. $10\log_{10}(16) \approx 12$ dB. Detection range is hard-won in radar.

items.prompt: An adjacent transmitter raises interference far above the thermal floor. To improve this link you should FIRST:
choices: Improve C/I via filtering, antenna nulling, or frequency coordination
items.explanation: The link is interference-limited ($I \gg N$). Raising power or lowering NF addresses noise, not interference — and power increases may worsen the shared environment. Reject the interferer instead.

items.prompt: What single change turns a one-way Friis link budget into the two-way radar range equation?
choices: Replace the receive antenna with a target RCS and apply the spreading loss twice
Convert all dBm values to dBW
items.explanation: The radar re-radiates off a target of cross-section $\sigma$ and the energy travels out and back, so spreading ($1/R^2$) applies twice ($1/R^4$) and the shared antenna gain squares.
